symbols: cross-topped staff — the power of Christ's sacrifice as the weapon against evil
shield with red cross — faith as the shield that extinguishes the fiery darts of the evil one
red cloak billowing — the blood of sacrifice and the mantle of divine authority
demons cowering beneath — the defeated forces of darkness
wings spread wide — celestial power and majesty
description: Archangel Michael stands triumphant at the center of the composition, wings spread wide, wearing ornate armor and a billowing red cloak. He holds a tall cross-topped staff in one hand and bears a shield with a red cross. Beneath his feet and around the rocky terrain, grotesque demonic creatures cower, writhe, and flee. In the upper right, a small figure of God the Father appears in a cloud with another angel. The landscape is rocky and barren, suggesting the boundary between heaven and the abyss. The composition conveys absolute victory of the heavenly warrior over the forces of evil.
theological_description: This altarpiece presents Archangel Michael in his primary biblical role as the champion of God's army against the forces of evil. Revelation describes the decisive battle: 'Michael and his angels fought against the dragon, and the dragon and his angels fought back. But he was not strong enough, and they lost their place in heaven' (Revelation 12:7-8). The cross-topped staff identifies the source of Michael's power — not his own might but the victory of Christ on the cross. As Revelation declares, the saints 'triumphed over him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony' (Revelation 12:11). The shield with its cross evokes Paul's spiritual warfare imagery: 'Take up the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one' (Ephesians 6:16). Daniel names Michael as 'the great prince who protects your people' (Daniel 12:1), the guardian of Israel in the cosmic struggle between good and evil. Jude records that 'even the archangel Michael, when he was disputing with the devil about the body of Moses, did not himself dare to condemn him, but said, The Lord rebuke you!' (Jude 1:9) — his authority derives entirely from God.

scripture_refs.reason: Michael and his angels fighting the dragon — the direct subject of this altarpiece
Michael as the great prince who protects God's people
The shield of faith extinguishing evil's arrows — Michael's cross-bearing shield
Victory through the blood of the Lamb — the cross atop Michael's staff
